trigger_phrases: go back where you came from
go back to where you came from
go back to where they came from

card.title: Indigenous America: 15,000 Years of History

timeline.event: Indigenous peoples settle the Americas — thousands of years before any European contact
European contact begins; Columbus reaches the Caribbean
Indian Removal Act forces mass relocation of tribes from ancestral lands
Dawes Act breaks up tribal land ownership, reducing Indigenous land by 90 million acres
Native Americans finally granted US citizenship — in their own ancestral homeland
~3.7 million Native Americans remain. Many nations maintain sovereign governments.

card.irony_highlight: Calling Indigenous people 'illegal' reverses 15,000 years of documented history. By any historical standard, they were here first.
card.empathy_angle: Imagine being told you don't belong in the land your ancestors have called home for 150 centuries.
sources: National Museum of the American Indian (Smithsonian)

rephrase_suggestions: Instead of 'go back to your country', try: 'I have concerns about immigration policy because...'
Instead of 'real Americans', try: 'US citizens' or 'people born here'

card.hook: What does the scientific record actually show?
card.title: Climate Change: The Scientific Consensus

timeline.event: Svante Arrhenius first calculates greenhouse effect of CO₂ — his math still holds
Keeling Curve begins tracking atmospheric CO₂; shows steady rise
NASA scientist James Hansen testifies to Congress: global warming is occurring
IPCC wins Nobel Peace Prize for climate research; 97% scientific consensus established
Hottest year on record. 12 of the 13 hottest years ever recorded occurred after 2010.

card.irony_highlight: The claim that 97% of climate scientists are coordinating a global hoax requires a conspiracy involving millions of researchers across 195 countries, many of whom are competing with each other.
card.empathy_angle: Coastal and island communities — many of them poor — face loss of their homes to rising seas regardless of the political debate.
sources: NOAA National Centers for Environmental Information

rephrase_suggestions: Instead of 'hoax', try: 'I'm skeptical about the causes of climate change because...'
Instead of 'climate scam', try: 'I question the proposed policy responses to climate change'

card.hook: What does research say about immigration and crime?
card.title: Immigration: History vs. Headlines

timeline.event: Irish immigrants labeled 'criminals' and 'subhuman' by US media and politicians
Chinese Exclusion Act — first major law to restrict immigration by ethnicity
Italian and Jewish immigrants called 'criminals' and 'racially inferior' by Congress
Japanese Americans — many born as US citizens — imprisoned in internment camps as 'threats'
Multiple studies confirm: immigrants (including undocumented) commit crimes at lower rates than native-born citizens

card.irony_highlight: The 'immigrant crime' narrative has been used against every major immigrant group in US history — Irish, Italian, Jewish, Chinese, Japanese, Latino. Each group later became 'real Americans.'
card.empathy_angle: Most immigrants leave everything they know seeking safety or opportunity. The journey itself is extraordinarily dangerous.
sources: National Academy of Sciences: The Integration of Immigrants into American Society

rephrase_suggestions: Instead of 'immigrants are criminals', try: 'I have concerns about border security because...'
Instead of 'deport them all', try: 'I believe immigration enforcement should prioritize...'

card.hook: How did vaccines change human life expectancy?
card.title: Vaccines: What the Data Shows

timeline.event: Edward Jenner creates first vaccine (smallpox); eventually saves ~5 million lives per year
Polio vaccine introduced. Polio paralyzed 35,000 Americans per year; now nearly eradicated worldwide.
Andrew Wakefield publishes fraudulent autism-vaccine study. Later retracted; Wakefield lost medical license.
Measles outbreaks rise in areas with low vaccine rates — a disease declared eliminated in 2000
WHO: vaccines prevent 4-5 million deaths per year. Autism rates unchanged by vaccine status in 95+ studies.

card.irony_highlight: The 1998 study linking vaccines to autism was fabricated — its author manipulated data for financial gain. Over 50 studies involving millions of children have found no link.
card.empathy_angle: Parents of children with autism deserve accurate information, not a scapegoat that delays finding real answers.

rephrase_suggestions: Instead of 'vaccines cause autism', try: 'I have questions about vaccine schedules and would like to discuss them with my doctor'
Instead of 'don't vaccinate', try: 'I want to understand more about the evidence before deciding'

card.title: Election Integrity: Following the Evidence

timeline.event: 2020 US election held. 60+ post-election lawsuits filed alleging fraud.
60+ courts — including judges appointed by Trump — dismiss fraud claims for lack of evidence
Trump's own AG William Barr: 'We have not seen fraud on a scale that could have affected the outcome'
Trump's own CISA director Chris Krebs calls 2020 'the most secure election in American history'
Arizona Republican-led Cyber Ninjas audit confirms Biden won Arizona
Fox News pays $787M to Dominion Voting Systems, admitting on-air claims were false

card.irony_highlight: Fox News privately acknowledged the fraud claims were false while publicly promoting them — a fact revealed in court documents and confirmed by an $787M settlement.
card.empathy_angle: Confidence in elections is foundational to democracy. The best way to protect it is with accurate information about how elections actually work.
sources: Dominion v. Fox News court documents

rephrase_suggestions: Instead of 'stolen election', try: 'I have concerns about specific aspects of election administration such as...'
Instead of 'voter fraud', try: 'I'd like to learn more about election security measures'

card.title: LGBTQ+: Science and History

timeline.event: Alfred Kinsey's research reveals same-sex attraction is common across human populations
American Psychiatric Association removes homosexuality from its list of disorders
American Medical Association opposes 'conversion therapy' as ineffective and harmful
US Supreme Court rules LGBTQ+ workers are protected under civil rights law
All major medical organizations — AMA, APA, AAP — affirm LGBTQ+ identities are natural human variation

card.irony_highlight: Same-sex behavior has been documented in 1,500+ animal species. The idea that it is 'unnatural' contradicts decades of biological research.
card.empathy_angle: LGBTQ+ youth who feel rejected are 8x more likely to attempt suicide. Acceptance from family cuts that risk dramatically.

rephrase_suggestions: Instead of 'groomer', try expressing specific policy concerns without attacking individuals' identities
Instead of 'agenda', try: 'I have concerns about specific policies such as...'

card.hook: Words have more impact than we expect
card.title: How Online Language Affects Real People

timeline.event: Studies show that exposure to dehumanizing language online correlates with increased real-world discrimination and violence.
UN report: hate speech on social media directly contributed to violence against Rohingya people in Myanmar.
Facebook's internal research found that inflammatory content drove higher engagement — and also more real-world conflict.
MIT study: false and inflammatory content spreads 6x faster on social media than accurate, measured information.
The words we post can reach millions in seconds — including people who belong to the groups being discussed.

card.irony_highlight: Content meant to express frustration often lands hardest on people who had no role in causing that frustration.
card.empathy_angle: Behind every post is a real audience. Some of them are directly affected by the way groups they belong to are talked about.
sources: UN Report on Hate Speech and Human Rights (2019)
MIT Media Lab: Language and Bias Research

rephrase_suggestions: Try expressing the specific concern without targeting a group of people
Ask: what outcome do you actually want from this post?

card.hook: What does the data show about racial disparities?
card.title: Systemic Racism: Reading the Data

timeline.event: Federal Housing Administration redlining codifies racial segregation in housing — affecting generational wealth
Voting Rights Act passes after documented, systematic disenfranchisement of Black voters
Studies show identical résumés with 'white-sounding' names get 50% more callbacks than 'Black-sounding' names
COVID-19 reveals stark disparities in healthcare outcomes along racial lines
Federal Reserve: White families have 6x the median wealth of Black families — a gap that has barely changed since the 1960s

card.irony_highlight: The wealth gap between white and Black families was largely created by specific government policies (redlining, GI Bill exclusions). Denying this history doesn't make the wealth gap disappear.
card.empathy_angle: Most people don't choose the circumstances they're born into. Structural barriers operate regardless of individual attitudes.
sources: Federal Reserve Survey of Consumer Finances
National Bureau of Economic Research (résumé studies)
Brookings Institution racial wealth gap research

rephrase_suggestions: Instead of 'racism is over', try: 'I think racial disparities have multiple causes including...'
Instead of 'playing the race card', try: 'Let's look at the data on this specific situation'
